1:
The chapter opens on the united monarchy at its height, and the witnesses first settle what the bare sentence means and why it stands where it does. The grammatical-historical commentators read it as a heading. Keil and Delitzsch explain that the whole chapter is a register — the chief ministers of state (vv.2–6) and the twelve officers set over the land (vv.7–20) — inserted to give an idea of the might and glory of the kingdom under Solomon, and belonging by its contents to the middle of the reign, since two of the officers had married daughters of Solomon (vv.11, 15) whom they could not have wedded in his earlier years. Charles Ellicott weighs the little word all: the emphasis laid upon reigning 'over all Israel' is characteristic of a writer who compiled the book after the disruption of the kingdom, when an undivided Israel had already become a memory. Adam Clarke, for his part, uses the verse to open the chapter's whole argument, laying out in order the officers, the daily provision, the peace of the dominions, and the wisdom that the following verses unfold.

The Reformed John Gill hears in the phrase a tribute to that wisdom. David his father was not at first king over all Israel but only over Judah, and Solomon's successors after Rehoboam ruled only a fragment; Solomon alone reigned by the consent of all, seated in the hearts of the whole people because of the wisdom for which the preceding chapter had made him famous. The note thus looks back to the gift of 1 Kings 3 as the ground of a united throne.

The Puritan Matthew Henry turns the throne into a lesson on office. Solomon was not merely called king but was confirmed and established king over all Israel, and — more to the point — 'he did the work and duty of a king, with the wisdom God had given him.' Those, Henry adds, preserve the name and honour of their place who mind its business and make conscience of it. The witnesses do not dispute. Together they present a kingdom briefly whole, its unity already marked as precious by the very writer who knew it would not last, and its king held up as a pattern of the diligence that high place requires — a glory that, as the same commentators will remember, foreshadows the greater and unbroken kingdom of the Son of David.
2:
The list of great officers begins, and the witnesses fasten on its striking first name and its striking first word. John Gill simply glosses the 'princes' as those in office about the king, holding the highest posts of honour and trust. Adam Clarke guards the English reader against a false note: these were the great, chief, or principal men, and 'none of them were princes in the common acceptation of the word.' The grammatical-historical commentators press further. Keil and Delitzsch observe that the head of the list is not the commander-in-chief, as under the warlike reign of David (2 Samuel 8:16), but, in keeping with the peaceful rule of Solomon, the administrator of the kingdom — a kind of prime minister; and Azariah is called ha-kohen not as 'the priest' but as the first among the king's confidential counsellors. Charles Ellicott notes the same order pointedly: whereas David's lists put the captain of the host first, here the peaceful offices of priests, scribes, and recorder stand foremost, a sign of the changed character of the reign.

The one real puzzle is the title 'the priest.' Ellicott identifies this Azariah with the son of Ahimaaz and grandson of Zadok named in 1 Chronicles 6:9–10, and holds that 'the priest' is added here by anticipation, since the text says below that Zadok and Abiathar were the priests. He then draws out the deeper history of the word Cohen: it retains traces of the old time when priesthood and the headship of the family were united, and so is applied to the sons of David in 2 Samuel 8:18 (which 1 Chronicles 18:17 paraphrases 'chief about the king'), even as David himself once wore the priestly ephod (2 Samuel 6:14). Keil records the rival construction of the Vulgate and Luther, which ties 'the priest' to Zadok rather than to Azariah, but rejects it against the accents.

The division, then, is narrow and grammatical: whether ha-kohen here means an administrator or a priest, and whether the title clings to Azariah or to his grandfather Zadok. Beneath it the witnesses agree on the larger picture Ellicott names — a court in which priestly and princely functions mingle, and in which the arts of peace, not of war, now take the first rank.
3:
The secretaries and the recorder are named, and the witnesses read the verse as a study in the continuity of government. John Gill notes that the father, Shisha, had been scribe only in David's time; being now dead, his two sons Elihoreph and Ahiah were both continued as secretaries of state, Solomon having more business for such an office than his father had. Adam Clarke renders the posts into the language of his own day: the scribes are secretaries to the king, and Jehoshaphat the recorder is the historiographer who chronicled the affairs of the kingdom — an office he had already held under David (2 Samuel 20:24).

The grammatical-historical commentators supply the archival background. Keil and Delitzsch identify these sopherim as secretaries of state who had held the same office under David, and Jehoshaphat the son of Ahilud as the chancellor of the previous reign (2 Samuel 8:17), refusing the loose rendering that would make him merely a passing figure. Charles Ellicott traces the family thread most fully: the Shisha here is the 'Shavsha' of 1 Chronicles 18:16 and the 'Sheva' of 2 Samuel 20:25, variations of one name, so that the office had become virtually hereditary. The scribe, he explains, was a high officer who issued the king's edicts and letters and acted in his name, like a Secretary of State; the recorder, or remembrancer, was the annalist who drew up and preserved the archives of the kingdom.

There is no quarrel here, and the witnesses draw a single lesson. The machinery of a literate, well-ordered state — a chancery to keep the records and a secretariat to publish the royal will — is set down almost in passing, and it is the same machinery that had served David. Matthew Henry, surveying the whole roll, had already caught its temper: several of Solomon's officers 'are the same that were in his father's time,' and Solomon, wise as he was, 'would not affect to be wiser than his father in this matter.' The continuity of trusted servants is presented as a mark of the king's own prudence and of a due respect for the memory of a faithful reign.
4:
Two names in this verse arrest the witnesses: Benaiah set over the host in Joab's room, and — more perplexing — Abiathar still called a priest though Solomon had deposed him. John Gill marks the first plainly: Benaiah the son of Jehoiada is general of the army in the room of Joab, the transfer that the executions of the foregoing chapter (1 Kings 2:35) had brought about. But it is the second name that draws the real labour.

The grammatical-historical commentators feel the difficulty at once. Keil and Delitzsch call it strange that Abiathar should be named as priest alongside Zadok, since Solomon had deposed him from the priestly office (1 Kings 2:27) and no later pardon can be imagined. Their solution, borrowed from Theodoret, distinguishes the two things the one word joins: Solomon took from Abiathar the arche, the exercise of the office, but not the hierosyne, the priestly dignity itself, 'because this was hereditary.' A man might be stripped of the function and yet retain the rank into which he had been born. Charles Ellicott reaches the same end by a vivid parallel: Abiathar, though disgraced and practically deposed, 'was still regarded theoretically as priest — much as Annas is called high priest in the Gospels — for the priesthood was properly for life.'

The witnesses do not divide; they converge on a single explanation of an apparent contradiction. The sacred writer has not blundered in listing a deposed man among the priests: he has preserved the exact truth that office and order are not the same thing, that Zadok now holds the active priesthood while Abiathar keeps the indelible character of it. The verse quietly seals the doom long before pronounced on the house of Eli, that God would raise up a faithful priest to walk before his anointed for ever (1 Samuel 2:35); Zadok, the faithful priest, now stands alone in the active charge, and Abiathar, of Eli's condemned line, retains only the empty honour of his birth. Even as the verse seats Benaiah where Joab once stood, it fixes Zadok where Abiathar once served, so that the peaceful administration of Solomon rests, the commentators observe, upon a settled and rightful order in both the sword and the sanctuary.
5:
Two sons of Nathan appear, and the witnesses spend their care on who this Nathan was and on the curious title borne by Zabud. John Gill gives the plain office: Azariah the son of Nathan was over the officers, that is, over the twelve purveyors soon to be named. The grammatical-historical commentators press the genealogy. Keil and Delitzsch insist that Zabud's title kohen does not mean, as Luther and others render it, that he was son of 'Nathan the priest'; it is explained by the words that follow, 'the king's friend,' and means a privy councillor, a confidential adviser. This Nathan, they add, is not the prophet but the son of David named in 2 Samuel 5:14, so that Azariah and Zabud were nephews of Solomon. Charles Ellicott argues the point at length and to the same conclusion: the title Cohen is expressly given to the sons of David in 2 Samuel 8:18, while Nathan the prophet always has 'the prophet' appended when first named in this book (1 Kings 1:8); therefore the father here is Nathan the king's brother, and Zabud is a royal cousin advanced to be the king's intimate.

The added phrase 'the king's friend' the witnesses read as a real office of the heart as well as of the state. Ellicott recalls that the same title had belonged to Hushai in David's day (2 Samuel 15:37; 16:16), and takes it to mark a special intimacy and wisdom, a trusted privy counsellor at the king's side. Where the Authorised Version renders Zabud 'principal officer,' Ellicott would keep the fuller sense — friend and confidant.

The commentators do not contend among themselves; their shared point is that Solomon set his own kindred in the nearest places of trust, over the officers and at his own right hand. It is presented neither as nepotism to be blamed nor as accident, but as the natural shape of an Eastern court, in which the king's brother's sons keep the officers and guard the king's ear — a picture the witnesses will complete when the officers themselves are counted in the next verses.
6:
The roll of ministers closes with two household officers, and the witnesses note that one of them carries a shadow over the reign. John Gill makes Ahishar the steward of the household. Keil and Delitzsch define the post more exactly as governor of the palace, the minister of the king's household, an office met with for the first time under Solomon (compare 1 Kings 16:9; 2 Kings 18:18; Isaiah 22:15). Adam Clarke calls him simply the king's chamberlain.

It is the second name, Adoniram over the tribute, that the grammatical-historical commentators dwell on, for it opens onto the darker underside of Solomon's splendour. Keil identifies him with the Adoram of 2 Samuel 20:24, chief overseer of the tributary service, who had held the same charge under David. Charles Ellicott draws the office out fully: the man 'over the levy' was the head of Solomon's great public works (1 Kings 5:14), the master of the forced labour by which the temple and the cities were raised. And Ellicott follows the thread to its bitter end — this same officer, most unpopular with those who had felt the burden of Solomon's magnificence, was stoned to death in the insurrection against Rehoboam (1 Kings 12:18). Clarke, comparing the post to a chancellor of the exchequer who gathered the taxes and tributes into the treasury, notes that he too had served in David's later years.

Ellicott adds a text-critical footnote from the Greek, which supplies an officer 'over the body-guard' omitted in the Hebrew, and wonders whether, after the rebellion of Adonijah had shown how easily that captaincy could become a quasi-independent power, no successor to Benaiah was appointed. He notes too that the same triad of offices — over the household, the scribe, and the recorder — reappears together in Hezekiah's court (2 Kings 18:18), a sign that the machinery Solomon set up outlasted his reign. The witnesses do not debate; but their combined reading gives the verse a double face. On its surface it is another line in an efficient administration; beneath, in the officer of the levy, they have marked the very grievance that would one day tear the kingdom in two, so that the minister of Solomon's glory becomes, in the end, the first casualty of its collapse.
7:
The twelve district officers are introduced, and the witnesses read the arrangement as a picture of a newly organized state. John Gill corrects a natural guess: the twelve answer not to the twelve tribes — for they had not each a tribe, but particular places within one — so much as to the twelve months, each officer taking his turn to provide. Adam Clarke agrees that the business was to furnish, each for a single month, the provisions consumed in the king's household (vv.22–23), and adds dryly that 'the task for such a daily provision was not an easy one.'

The grammatical-historical commentators define the office. Keil and Delitzsch take the netsibim to be not chamberlains or stewards of the royal domains but general receivers of taxes, chief tax-collectors, who levied the king's dues — paid in the East, then as now, mostly in the produce of the land rather than in money — and delivered them to the royal kitchen. Charles Ellicott presses the historical significance: the collection of revenue in kind maintained the court, household, and guards, and the office was one of high dignity, since in two cases (vv.11, 15) its holders were married into the royal house. Most tellingly, he observes that the provinces — nine west and three east of Jordan — 'coincide only in a few cases with the lands assigned to the several tribes,' the old tribal division of territory having by now largely become obsolete for such purposes, even where (1 Chronicles 27:16–22) it lingered for the chieftainship of men and the levy of war.

Matthew Henry, surveying the whole scheme, draws its prudence into the open. To bring the king's food from afar was good husbandry, like the virtuous woman who 'bringeth her food from afar' (Prov. 31:14); to divide the trust among many hands was wise, that no man might be continually burdened or grow exorbitantly rich by it; and the traffic in provisions spread wealth into every corner of the land, encouraging industry even in the tribes most remote from the court. The witnesses agree in reading the verse not as dry bureaucracy but as the visible order of a well-governed realm, in which the very machinery of provision serves the good of the whole people.
8:
The catalogue of districts begins with mount Ephraim, and the witnesses pause first on a peculiarity of the names. Keil and Delitzsch and John Gill both remark that in five cases the officer is given not his own name but his father's, so that we read 'the son of Hur,' 'the son of Dekar,' and the like. Keil weighs the suggestion that the proper names have simply dropped out, finds it improbable that this should have happened five times, and prefers the view that these men bore their fathers' names, with Ben prefixed, as their own — Ben-hur, Ben-dekar — after the analogy of a Ben-hanan in 1 Chronicles 4:20. Gill takes the same line, that the fathers' names are given as being well known; and Ellicott had already traced the oddity to a defect in the ancient archives from which the chapter was drawn, where in five cases the officer's own name had dropped away and only the patronymic survived. So even the imperfections of the record become, for the commentators, a token of its antiquity and its fidelity to an old written source.

On the district itself the commentators are warm. Keil calls the mountains of Ephraim, here the hill-country of that tribe, among the most fruitful portions of Palestine (see Joshua 17:14–15). Charles Ellicott describes it more lyrically as one of the most fertile and beautiful regions in the land, the high central territory of Ephraim, surrounding the city of Shechem, which lies in a rich plain between Mount Ebal and Mount Gerizim, and including the strong site of the future Samaria. He points the reader to the blessing of Moses upon Joseph, with its 'precious things of the earth and fulness thereof' (Deut. 33:13–17), as the Scripture's own witness to the wealth of the ground.

There is no dispute in so plain a notice, and the witnesses let the significance rest in the arrangement itself. That the roll of Solomon's larders should open with the garden-heartland of Ephraim, the most jealous and populous of the northern tribes, is quietly fitting: the very region that would one day head the revolt against the house of David is here first named among the districts that fed the king's table, a token of the brief season in which the whole land, from its richest hills, served one throne in peace.
9:
The second district lies in the old Danite country toward the sea, and the witnesses give it chiefly to geography, yet not without a memory that lights up the list. Keil and Delitzsch locate the towns as best they can: Makaz is named only here and its site is lost, but it must be sought in the tribe of Dan; Shaalbim survives in the village of Selbit; Bethshemesh is the present Ain-Shems (Joshua 15:10); and Elon, distinguished by the epithet Beth-hanan from the Ajalon of Joshua 19:42, has not been certainly identified. John Gill adds only that Makaz stood in the tribe of Dan, on its borders.

Charles Ellicott fills in the history the bare names carry. This second division lay in the maritime plain to the north-west of Judah, assigned by lot to Dan but through most of the earlier history held by the Philistines; its cities Shaalbim, Elon, and Beth-shemesh are noted together in Joshua 19:41–43. Of them all, he observes, the one truly known to history is Beth-shemesh — 'the first resting-place of the Ark' when the Philistines restored it (1 Samuel 6:12–21). That single note transfigures the district: the plain where the milch-kine drew the cart of God home to Israel, and where the men of the city were smitten for looking into the ark (1 Samuel 6:19), is now a quiet province rendering its month's provision to Solomon. Keil adds a small textual caution about the last town, Elon-beth-hanan, which the Septuagint would join to Beth-hanan by inserting a word the Hebrew does not warrant; he declines the emendation, preferring to leave the obscure name as it stands rather than mend the text to fit a guess.

The witnesses do not differ, and the verse yields its meaning through their united topography. What had been debated borderland between Israel and Philistia, a coastal march that Dan could never wholly hold, is here folded peaceably into the king's revenue system. The commentators let the contrast speak: the frontier of old struggle and of the ark's homecoming has become, under Solomon, simply the second name in a list of larders — the fruit of the peace that his father's wars had won.
10:
The third district reaches into the lowland of Judah, and the witnesses read it through the places whose names still carry the memory of David's youth. John Gill places Aruboth in the tribe of Judah, judging by the towns that follow. Keil and Delitzsch concede that Arubboth occurs nowhere else, so its site can only be inferred from its neighbours; but Sochoh shows the district to lie in Judah, and the land of Hepher — not to be confused with Gath-hepher in Zebulun — was the territory of one of the Canaanite kings whom Joshua defeated (Joshua 12:17), probably in the plain. Of the two towns called Sochoh in Judah, one on the mountains and one in the lowland (Joshua 15:48), Keil inclines to the one in the plain, since it is here joined with the land of Hepher and the district plainly lay in the lowland country toward the sea.

Charles Ellicott gives the district its historical colour. It lay, he says, in the country of the Philistines, part of the territory assigned to Judah; Sochoh is the town of Joshua 15:35, noticed in 1 Samuel 17:1–3 as close to the field of battle on which David slew Goliath; and Hepher is an old Amorite city conquered by Joshua, which by 'a curious survival' still gave its name to the whole district. The valley where the shepherd-boy felled the giant is thus quietly named among Solomon's provinces, a reminder of the deliverance out of which the peaceful kingdom grew.

The witnesses have no quarrel over so plain a notice, and they do not force the geography beyond what the text will bear; where a site is lost, Keil says so, and where a name survives from an older world, Ellicott marks the survival. Yet the cumulative effect of their reading is not dry. District by district the commentators are showing how the land of Israel's hardest-won battles — Philistia's plain, the Amorite's city, the field of Goliath — had been gathered, under the son of David, into a single ordered economy of peace, each ancient battlefield now measured out as ground that feeds the king.
11:
With the fourth district a new note enters the list — the governor is a king's son-in-law — and the witnesses attend both to the coast he ruled and to the marriage that bound him to the throne. John Gill identifies the region of Dor with Dor and her towns, belonging to the half-tribe of Manasseh. Keil and Delitzsch map it as the high range of Dor (Joshua 12:23), the strip of coast on the Mediterranean below the promontory of Carmel, where the city of Dor survives in the village of Tantura, nine miles north of Caesarea; and they note that Ben-Abinadab was probably a son of David's elder brother, and so Solomon's cousin, before he became also his son-in-law by marrying Taphath.

Charles Ellicott confirms the geography — the fourth division still lying along the coast, but northward under Mount Carmel, in the territory of Manasseh — and recalls that Dor had once formed part of the northern confederacy under Jabin (Joshua 11:2) before it was conquered and given to Manasseh (Joshua 17:11). The commentators do not make much of a quarrel over the exact bounds; Keil cautions only that it is not so clear as some suppose whether the district reached down into the plain of Sharon.

What the witnesses do underline is the significance of the marriage. Twice in this roll (here and at v.15) an officer is wedded to a daughter of Solomon, and the commentators read it as no idle detail. It fixes the date of the list in the later years of the reign, when the king had grown daughters to give; and it shows the policy by which the crown knit its provincial governors to itself, binding the administration together with ties of blood. Matthew Henry, noticing the same alliances, judged them no disparagement but a wise preference: 'better match with the officers of their father's court that were Israelites than with the sons of princes that were strangers to the covenant of promise.' The witnesses thus turn a line of geography into a glimpse of statecraft and of covenant prudence together.
12:
The fifth district is the great plain of Jezreel, and the witnesses, naming its cities, find themselves reciting the very map of Israel's history. John Gill sets Taanach, Megiddo, and all Bethshean in the tribe of Manasseh. Keil and Delitzsch reckon Baana the son of Ahilud most likely a brother of Jehoshaphat the chancellor of v.3, and trace the district from the cities on the southern edge of the plain of Jezreel down to the Jordan — Taanach and Megiddo surviving in Taanuk and Lejun, and the whole territory of Bethshean, the later Beisan, reaching to where the valley opens into the Jordan valley.

Charles Ellicott makes this the largest and most important division and lets its history unroll. Taanach, Megiddo, and Beth-shean were Canaanite cities never taken by Manasseh but made tributary (Joshua 17:11; Judges 1:27); Taanach and Megiddo ring in the song of Deborah (Judges 5:19); Megiddo would see the death of Ahaziah (2 Kings 9:27) and the fall of Josiah (2 Kings 23:29); Beth-shean is the city on whose wall the Philistines hung the body of Saul (1 Samuel 31:12); and Abel-meholah, the birthplace of Elisha (1 Kings 19:16), lies in the record of Gideon's rout of Midian (Judges 7:22). Ellicott even recovers the right reading of the last name as Jokmeam, a Levitical city of Ephraim (1 Chronicles 6:68).

The witnesses do not dispute the ground, and their agreement produces the verse's peculiar power. This plain — 'the garden and battle-field of Northern Palestine,' in Ellicott's phrase — is at once the scene of Deborah's triumph and Gideon's, of Saul's shame and Josiah's death, the field to which the history keeps returning to be decided. Here, in Solomon's day, it is none of these things: it is simply the fifth and richest of the king's larders, its battle-plain turned wholly to harvest. The commentators, by piling name on remembered name, let the reader feel how deep and how brief was the peace that made a granary of the ground where Israel so often bled.
13:
The sixth district crosses the Jordan into Bashan, and the witnesses gather around its sixty walled cities and the giant-kingdom they once had been. John Gill places Ben-geber in Ramoth-gilead, a city of the tribe of Gad and a city of refuge. Keil and Delitzsch fix Ramoth in Gilead (Joshua 20:8) and identify the towns of Jair with the sixty great fortified cities of the region of Argob, all of them cities of the kingdom of Og of Bashan which Israel took under Moses (Deut. 3:4). Charles Ellicott renders Argob 'the rocky region,' afterwards the Greek Trachonitis, the land of Og 'covered with great cities,' taken by Jair the son of Manasseh and called Havoth-jair, the towns of Jair (Deut. 3:13–14); and he notes that Ramoth-gilead, a Levitical city and city of refuge, would become famous in the later Syrian wars (1 Kings 22:3; 2 Kings 8:28).

Here the company gains a genuine outlier. Adam Clarke, catching at the phrase 'threescore great cities with walls and brazen bars,' turns aside from geography to literature: these were fortified cities whose gates and bars were plated with brass, and he quotes at length Virgil's picture of Pyrrhus hewing through the brazen doors of Priam's palace — 'the solid timbers from the portal tore, and rent from every hinge the brazen door.' It is a classical embellishment none of the others attempt, lighting the ancient strongholds with a page of the Aeneid.

The witnesses do not disagree on the substance; their combined note simply enlarges the district's grandeur. The very land of the last of the giants — Og, the remnant of the Rephaim, whose iron bedstead Moses had recorded (Deut. 3:11) — the rocky region of sixty brazen-barred fortresses, is here one province among twelve, rendering its month's provision like the rest. And the commentators mark, without labouring it, that Ramoth-gilead — now a peaceful larder — would in a later and sadder reign become the battlefield where kings of Israel fell, so that the peace of Solomon is felt against the wars that framed it.
14:
The seventh district is the country round Mahanaim, and the witnesses cannot name the place without recalling the two great nights of its history. John Gill remembers it as the city 'where both Ishbosheth and David sometimes dwelt,' and infers from the officer's charge that it and the land about it must have been very fruitful, since one man was to furnish the king a whole month's provision from it. Keil and Delitzsch place Mahanaim as a fortified and important city north of the Jabbok, on the border of Gad, perhaps preserved in the ruin of Mahneh (Joshua 13:26; Genesis 32:2).

Charles Ellicott unfolds the name, which means 'the camps' or 'the two hosts.' It was, he says, the scene of Jacob's angelic vision on his return to Canaan, when the angels of God met him and he called the place Mahanaim (Genesis 32:1–2); afterward it became the seat of Ishbosheth's short-lived government (2 Samuel 2:8), and the place where David established himself when he fled from Absalom (2 Samuel 17:24) and received the large supplies that Barzillai and other chiefs brought him (2 Samuel 17:27). The name itself, 'the two camps,' Jacob had coined for the meeting of the host of God with his own company; and the town of the two camps had since sheltered both the house of Saul in its brief resistance and the fleeing David in the bitterest of his exiles, before it welcomed him home again across the Jordan (2 Samuel 19:31).

The witnesses hold no debate; they let the layered memory do the work. Gill's plain inference about fruitfulness and Ellicott's rich history meet in a single point — that the very ground where angels once encamped about Jacob, and where David once found refuge and bread in his darkest hour, now sends its bread untroubled to the table of Jacob's greatest son. What had been a place of encampment in flight and fear is folded, under Solomon, into the settled peace of the realm, so that the commentators quietly turn a district-name into a reminder of the God who had camped with the patriarch and provided for the exiled king, and now crowned the line with rest.
15:
The eighth district is Naphtali in the far north, and the witnesses mark both its governor's likely pedigree and the second royal marriage in the list. John Gill notes plainly that Ahimaaz made his monthly provision annually out of the tribe of Naphtali. Keil and Delitzsch suggest that this Ahimaaz may be the son of Zadok known from David's history (2 Samuel 15:27; 17:17), and they add that, like Ben-Abinadab before him, he had a daughter of Solomon — Basmath — to wife. They are careful to bound the district against Thenius's over-large estimate: it did not run indefinitely into the north, for the territory of Asher, which had its own prefect, lay along Naphtali's western side (Joshua 19:24–31).

Charles Ellicott describes the eighth division as the upper valley of the Jordan, south of Mount Hermon, taking in part of the north-west shore of the sea of Gennesaret and the waters of Merom. Within it, he says, lay Hazor, once the head of the northern Canaanite confederacy, and the Levitical city of refuge Kedesh-Naphtali (Joshua 19:37; Judges 4:6) — the town from which Barak was summoned to the war of Deborah. Keil is careful, against an over-large estimate, not to stretch the district indefinitely northward into Coele-Syria, since Asher, with its own prefect, ran up the western side of Naphtali to the very northern boundary of Canaan.

The witnesses do not divide, and the verse gains its interest from the convergence of their notes. If Keil is right that the officer is Zadok's fleet-footed son, then the young runner who once bore the tidings of Absalom's defeat to David (2 Samuel 18:19) is now a settled governor and the king's son-in-law, provisioning the northern march that reaches to Hermon. Twice over, in this district and the fourth, the commentators have watched Solomon bind his frontier officers to the crown by marriage; and here the binding reaches the utmost north, to the land of Hazor and Kedesh, so that the tribe most exposed to the nations is drawn as firmly as any into the peace and the provision of the king's house.
16:
The ninth district is Asher on the north-western seaboard, and the witnesses read it through the tribe's old failure and the governor's honoured father. John Gill recalls that Asher was a very plentiful tribe, famous especially for its oil, and guesses that this Baanah was the son of Hushai the Archite, David's faithful friend, perhaps 'promoted for his sake.' Keil and Delitzsch make the same identification more firmly — Baanah the son of Hushai, probably the faithful counsellor who stood by David in Absalom's revolt (2 Samuel 15:32; 17:5) — and confess that the second place-name, Bealoth or Aloth, is quite unknown, warning only that it cannot be the Baaloth of the Negeb of Judah.

Charles Ellicott sets the district along the Tyrian border, stretching north from Mount Carmel, first along the coast and then behind the ranges of Lebanon; and he recalls the tribe's ancient shortcoming — that Asher 'did not occupy the territory assigned them' (Joshua 19:24–30) but dwelt among the native inhabitants and mingled with them (Judges 1:31–32). Aloth he leaves unidentified, noting only that Josephus places this province on the coast near Achzib. Gill's mention of the tribe's oil recalls the blessing of Moses, that Asher should be 'acceptable to his brethren' and dip his foot in oil (Deut. 33:24) — the fatness of its coast now flowing, like the rest, to the king's table.

The witnesses have no real dispute here, and their shared reading gives the verse a quiet double interest. On the one hand it shows the seed of David's loyalty bearing fruit into the next generation: as in Asher, so through the list, the sons of the men who were faithful to David — Hushai's son here, Zadok's perhaps at Naphtali — are found keeping the provinces, the crown remembering its friends. On the other hand the tribe whose failure of faith had once left it 'dwelling among the Canaanites' is now, oil-rich and settled, rendering its portion to the king like the rest, so that even Israel's half-obedience of the days of the Judges is, for a season, overtaken by the fulness of Solomon's peace.
17:
The tenth district is Issachar, and the witnesses use its brief notice to show how Solomon's provinces overlapped and absorbed the older tribal map. John Gill reads it simply: Jehoshaphat the son of Paruah had the whole tribe of Issachar at his command, to make out of it a month's provision for the king once a year, like the officers before and after him. Keil and Delitzsch sharpen the boundaries, for Issachar's portion lay in the plain of Jezreel, where it met the fifth district: this officer had the tribe's territory except the cities of Taanach, Megiddo, and Bethshean, which lay in its southern part but were allotted to Manasseh and, by v.12, were put under Baanah's care. And they raise a further possibility — that Zebulun, which is given no prefect of its own in the list, may have belonged, wholly or in its southern part, to this district or to Ahimaaz in Naphtali.

Charles Ellicott agrees that the tenth division, the territory of Issachar lying north of Manasseh and taking in part of the great plain of Esdraelon, 'must have been so closely connected with the fifth division that the frontiers could hardly be discerned.' The witnesses, then, are chiefly concerned to keep the reader from imagining neat tribal blocks: the districts interpenetrate, one tribe's cities are governed with another's, and a whole tribe like Zebulun can slip in unnamed under a neighbour. Keil weighs whether Zebulun, wanting a prefect of its own, was parted between this district and Ahimaaz in Naphtali, its southern portion falling to Jehoshaphat and its northern to the officer above — the tribes of Jacob's blessing quietly reshuffled for the practical ends of the crown.

There is no debate in so plain a matter, and the commentators do not manufacture one; but their careful untangling carries the same lesson that has been building through the list. The administrative geography of Solomon is not the geography of Joshua's lots. The old inheritances endure in name and memory, yet the working kingdom has been redrawn across them for the practical ends of revenue and provision — a quiet sign, the witnesses let us feel, of how far the loose tribal confederacy of the Judges has now become a single, centrally governed state.
18:
The eleventh district is Benjamin, and the witnesses, with little to detain them in the single name, dwell on the tribe's importance and on distinguishing this Shimei from another of ill fame. John Gill's chief concern is exactly that: Shimei the son of Elah is 'so described, to distinguish him from that Shimei that cursed David,' who was of the same tribe of Benjamin. Keil and Delitzsch add the likelier link — that this Shimei is possibly the one named in 1 Kings 1:8 among those who stood loyally with Solomon and would not side with Adonijah.

Charles Ellicott supplies the weight the bare notice lacks. The eleventh division, the territory of Benjamin — properly including Jerusalem itself — though small, is 'singularly strong and populous,' taking in Jericho, Bethel, Gibeon, and Ramah, stretching from Judah to Ephraim and commanding the centre of the high land of what would afterward be the kingdom of Judah. A little tribe, but the hinge of the whole country, holding the strong places — Jericho at the gate of the Jordan, Gibeon and Ramah on the northern approaches — that guarded the way to the capital.

The witnesses do not contend, and the verse yields its interest through their two small observations laid side by side. Gill's care to name the right Shimei is not pedantry: it quietly reminds the reader that the tribe which had produced the man who flung stones and curses at the fleeing David (2 Samuel 16:5) has also produced a man trusted enough to govern the royal heartland and, if Keil is right, the very Shimei who refused to join Adonijah and kept Solomon's side at the accession (1 Kings 1:8). The same Benjamin that harboured David's cruellest reviler now furnishes one of his son's twelve stewards. In so brief a compass the commentators let the grace of the settled kingdom appear — old enmities overtaken, the strong central tribe bound in loyal service, and a name once shadowed by a curse now standing clean in the roll of those who feed the king.
19:
The twelfth district covers the vast land east of Jordan, and the witnesses grapple both with its extent and with a famously obscure closing clause. John Gill takes the country of Gilead to mean the whole trans-Jordan territory of Reuben, Gad, and half-Manasseh, excepting the portions already assigned to Ben-geber and Ahinadab. Keil and Delitzsch read it the same way — the entire Israelite land beyond the Jordan, the country of Sihon and Og (Deut. 34:1), save those two earlier districts — and construe the difficult phrase as 'one president was it who was in the land,' meaning that, for all its great extent, this region had but a single prefect over it.

Charles Ellicott presses the harder problem in the text. He notes that the Hebrew 'he was the only officer in the land' yields very little sense, since every district had but one governor; and he prefers the reading preserved in the Greek, which supplies a governor 'in the land of Judah.' The point is weighty, for in the whole enumeration the royal tribe of Judah is otherwise altogether omitted. Ellicott judges it hardly likely that Judah should have been left outside the revenue system, and suggests that Azariah, who was over the officers and resident at court (v.5), may have been its territorial governor; he adds that in some Greek manuscripts the verses on the horses' provender (vv.27–28) follow directly here.

So the witnesses divide, gently, over what the last clause means. Keil and Gill take it at face value — one officer for the whole broad east — while Ellicott hears in it, or behind it, the lost mention of a thirteenth governor for Judah. The disagreement is textual, not doctrinal, and both sides serve the same end: to account honestly for the pastoral eastern march on the borders of Moab, and to notice the curious silence about the home province, whether that silence means Judah was governed directly from the court or that a name has fallen from the text. The commentators leave the reader with the twelvefold order complete and only its one true seam exposed.
20:
The account of the officers closes with a burst of national blessedness, and the witnesses hear in it the ancient promises coming true. John Gill reads Judah and Israel 'many as the sand which is by the sea' as the fruit of God's favour — a people blessed with fruitful families, untouched by pestilence or war — and finds in it the fulfilment of the word to Abraham that his seed should be as the sand of the sea (Genesis 22:17), even while he looks beyond it to Christ's spiritual subjects in the latter day. Keil and Delitzsch say the same of the history: the words show that the promise given to the patriarchs (Genesis 32:12) had now been kept, and they note that the ancient versions carried the account of Solomon's greatness straight on into the following chapter.

The Puritan Matthew Henry lifts the verse highest. Such a kingdom, he says, Israel never saw before or after; and never was that kingdom 'so glorious a type of the kingdom of the Messiah as it was then,' the account here answering the prophecy of that psalm which is 'for Solomon' but looks to Christ (Ps. 72:8–11). The eating and drinking and making merry he takes not as luxury but as the contented enjoyment of settled peace, every man secure in the plenty God had given. The prophecy he has in view promises the King's dominion 'from sea to sea, and from the river unto the ends of the earth' (Ps. 72:8), so that the census-note becomes a first line of that royal psalm made visible.

The witnesses do not dispute; they harmonize into a single reading in two registers. Historically, the sand-like multitude marks the moment when the Abrahamic promise of a numberless people stood visibly fulfilled, the low estate of the sojourning patriarchs answered by a nation too many to count. Typically — and here Gill and Henry speak with one voice — that fulfilled promise and that merry peace are but the shadow of a greater kingdom, the innumerable multitude of the true Israel rejoicing under great David's greater Son. The bare census-note becomes, in their hands, a window onto both the faithfulness of God to the fathers and the glory to which Solomon's peace could only point.
21:
The reach of the empire is now measured, and the witnesses set its splendour against both the covenant to Abraham and the sober law of how such empires rise and fall. John Gill reads Solomon reigning 'over all kingdoms' as dominion over all the peoples round about, who stood in awe of him and brought presents and tribute in acknowledgment of his sway. Adam Clarke traces the bounds — from the Euphrates on the east to the land of the Philistines on the west and the frontier of Egypt on the south — so that Syria, Damascus, Moab, and Ammon were his tributaries, and concludes that 'thus he appears to have possessed all the land that God covenanted with Abraham to give to his posterity' (compare Genesis 15:18). Keil and Delitzsch add the philological point that 'bringing presents is equivalent to paying tribute' (2 Samuel 8:2).

Charles Ellicott gives the fullest and most searching account. The dominion ran south to Philistia and the border of Egypt, east to the Euphrates as far as Tiphsah, north far beyond Damascus; the Syrian kingdoms, like Tyre, were allies on a footing of dependence rather than strict tributaries. But he insists on the nature of such a realm: it was not an organized monarchy but the supremacy of a dominant kingdom over tributaries gathered round, 'the kings on this side the river' who 'brought presents.' Such an empire, he warns, 'would rise rapidly, and as rapidly fall to pieces,' and Solomon's was sustained less by military power than by wealth, policy, and his own personal ascendancy.

The Puritan Matthew Henry keeps the note of glory, but grounds it in prophecy and in the labours of two reigns: the dominion 'from sea to sea' answers the psalm of Solomon that speaks of Christ (Ps. 72:8–11), and what David won by successful war Solomon made easy and reasonable by his wisdom. So the witnesses hold two truths together without collision. The empire is the visible keeping of the land-promise to Abraham and a bright type of Messiah's universal reign; yet, as Ellicott soberly marks, it is an empire of the old Eastern kind, resting on one man's ascendancy — glorious, God-given, and, apart from the greater King it foreshadows, fragile.
22:
The daily provision of the court is set down in staggering figures, and the witnesses turn arithmetic into a portrait of Solomon's magnificence. John Gill begins with the measure: the thirty measures of fine flour were cors — the cor being the same as the homer, which he reckons at some seventy-five gallons — so that the mind may grasp how vast a quantity was consumed in a single day. Keil and Delitzsch tally the whole: thirty cors of fine meal and sixty of ordinary, the cor holding ten ephahs; and, working from the bread that such flour would yield — some twenty-eight thousand pounds — they estimate, at two pounds of bread a head, a court of about fourteen thousand persons fed from the king's stores.

Adam Clarke presses the same computation further, and higher. He lists the provision item by item and, citing the older reckonings, reports that Vilalpandus computed the number fed daily at the palace at no fewer than forty-eight thousand six hundred, and Calvisius, from the consumption of food, at fifty-four thousand — 'these must have included all his guards, each of whom received a ration from the king's store.' Charles Ellicott is more cautious about the exact size of the measure, which he says is variously estimated — from eighty-six gallons down to forty-two — but agrees that 'in any case the quantity is very large,' indicating a household, court, and body-guard numbering probably several thousands, and warns the reader not to build too confidently on any one reckoning.

The witnesses do not quarrel over the principle, only over the multiplier, and their combined effect is to make the reader feel the scale rather than settle the sum. Whether fourteen thousand mouths or fifty, the point stands that a single day's bread at Solomon's table would provender a town. The commentators offer it neither as mere boasting nor as bare statistic, but as the concrete measure of the peace and plenty already praised — the visible weight, in flour and meal, of a kingdom so blessed that its capital could be fed daily like an army in the field, and want nothing.
23:
The meat of the royal table is now numbered, and the witnesses give most of their labour to the game animals whose names are hard to fix. John Gill notes only that the ten fat oxen were such as were kept up in the stall and fattened. Keil and Delitzsch complete the tally — ten fatted oxen, twenty from the pasture driven straight from grazing to slaughter, a hundred sheep, besides different kinds of game — and read it as of a piece with the bread of the preceding verse, the flesh in due proportion to the flour.

Adam Clarke, the philologist of the company, works through the Hebrew word by word. The 'sheep' (tson) comprehend both sheep and goats; the 'harts' are the deer; the 'roebucks' (tsebi) he takes for the gazelle or antelope; the 'fallow deer' (yachmur) for the buffalo; and the 'fatted fowl' (barburim abusim) he derives, following Michaelis, from a root meaning the open field or desert, so that the word may cover all the wild creatures — beasts as well as birds — taken by hunting, as opposed to the domesticated. He concludes that 'Solomon's table was spread with all the necessaries and delicacies which the house or the field could afford.' Charles Ellicott is content to leave the exact meaning of each term open, holding that 'the harts, roebucks, and the like, whatever the exact meaning of each word may be, evidently denote the wild game as distinct from the herds and flocks,' while the 'fatted fowl' signifies dainty food generally.

So the witnesses differ only in how far to press the lexicon — Clarke chasing each species to its root, Ellicott resting in the plain sense — and agree entirely on the substance. The verse rounds out the picture of a court fed from stall and pasture, hill and desert, the tame and the wild together. The commentators read it not as gluttony but as the abundance of a land at rest, in which every kind of creature the country yields is brought to a table that, as the next verses will say, lacked nothing — a plenty that is itself a testimony to the peace God had given.
24:
The ground of all this plenty is now given — dominion and peace — and the witnesses fasten on a geographical phrase that quietly betrays when the book was written. John Gill reads it plainly: Solomon had dominion 'over all the region on this side the river,' that is, beyond the Euphrates in the sense the writer intends, and this accounts for the plenty of provisions and the revenue that upheld so great a table. Keil and Delitzsch press the idiom. The Hebrew means 'over all the other side of the river,' the land west of the Euphrates; and this way of speaking, they argue, 'is to be explained from the fact that the author of our books, who was living in exile on the other side of the Euphrates, describes the extent of Solomon's kingdom taking that as his starting-point.' Tiphsah, the north-eastern limit, they identify with the wealthy city of Thapsacus, and Azzah with Gaza in the south-west.

Charles Ellicott reaches the same conclusion and states it as sharply. The Authorised Version's 'on this side the river,' though it points to the right country west of the Euphrates, is literally incorrect: the words mean 'on the further side of the river,' seen from the standpoint of Babylon (compare the usage in Ezra 4:6; 6:6), and so 'indicate composition at the time of the Exile,' or at least when the Babylonian empire so dominated the East as to fix its geographical nomenclature.

The witnesses do not divide over the sense; they unite in a striking observation. A single preposition, describing Solomon's dominion as lying 'beyond the river,' shows that the man recording his glory looked out upon the world from the far side of the Euphrates, from the land of the captivity. And the verse ends on the word the whole chapter has been reaching toward — 'he had peace on all sides round about him.' The commentators let the two facts stand together: an exile, remembering from Babylon the peace of the great king, so that Solomon's untroubled realm is described by one who had himself lost such peace, and the note of tranquillity is heard, poignantly, across the ruin that came after.
25:
The peace of the reign is now pictured in its homeliest and most enduring image — every man under his own vine and fig tree — and the witnesses read it at once as history and as prophecy. John Gill takes it of a people dwelling safely, without fear of injury to person or property from any enemy, and adds that this security 'is, and will be, more abundantly fulfilled in Christ, the antitype of Solomon.' Adam Clarke gives the social reality behind the emblem: the people 'were no longer obliged to dwell in fortified cities for fear of their enemies; they spread themselves over all the country, which they everywhere cultivated, and had always the privilege of eating the fruits of their own labours.'

Keil and Delitzsch supply the phrase's afterlife in Scripture. To sit every man under his vine and fig tree expresses the undisturbed enjoyment of the land's costly produce (2 Kings 18:31), and it is therefore taken up by the prophets as the very figure of the happiness of the Messianic age (Micah 4:4; Zechariah 3:10), when neighbour shall call neighbour under the vine and the fig tree. The reach 'from Dan even to Beersheba' they note as the standing formula for the whole extent of the land (Judges 20:1), so that the peace is as wide as Israel itself, unbroken from the northern border to the southern.

The witnesses do not contend; they build one meaning in two storeys. On the ground floor is Solomon's actual peace — an unwalled, cultivated land, every family secure in its own harvest from the northern border to the southern. Above it the commentators, with one accord, see the figure reaching up: the same prophets who borrowed this verse to paint the days of Messiah teach us to read Solomon's rest as the shadow of a deeper and lasting peace under great David's greater Son. Gill and Keil together let the fig-tree of Solomon's reign become the fig-tree of the Gospel, so that the historian's picture of a nation at ease is quietly transfigured into a promise still to be kept in full.
26:
The king's cavalry is numbered, and the witnesses divide their attention between a knotty textual difficulty and a grave point of the Law. The difficulty is the figure itself: forty thousand stalls here against four thousand in the parallel place. John Gill offers several reconciliations — that arbaim (forty) may be a copyist's slip for arbah (four); or that this verse counts the stalls, ten to a stable, where 2 Chronicles 9:25 counts the four thousand stables; or that here are reckoned all the stalls throughout the kingdom, there only those at Jerusalem. Keil and Delitzsch cut the knot more simply: 'forty thousand is an old copyist's error for four thousand,' for the fourteen hundred chariots of 1 Kings 10:26 suit four thousand horses well and forty thousand not at all. Adam Clarke canvasses the versions — the Septuagint's 'four thousand mares,' the Syriac and Arabic omitting the verse — and settles for a sober medium: four thousand stalls, fourteen hundred chariots, twelve thousand horsemen.

Charles Ellicott agrees that four thousand should be read, but turns from the number to its meaning. This multiplication of horses and horsemen, he observes, was expressly forbidden to the future king (Deut. 17:16) and yet foretold by Samuel at the founding of the monarchy (1 Samuel 8:11–12). Israel's armies had hitherto been mainly of infantry — in Joshua 11:9 the captured chariots and horses were not used but destroyed at God's command — so that 'now, as it would seem for the first time, this provision of the ancient law was set aside, and Solomon's empire assumed the character of other great Oriental monarchies.'

The witnesses do not quarrel over the correction; all grant a corruption in the numbers and read four thousand. Their deeper point is Ellicott's. Beneath the glitter of the cavalry lies the quiet setting-aside of a plain command, the king trusting to horses as the nations did. The commentators, faithful to their text, let the reader see in the very emblem of Solomon's strength the first shadow of the declension that would follow — a splendour bought at the price of a forgotten word of God.
27:
The narrative returns to the twelve officers to close the account of the provision, and the witnesses read the repetition as deliberate and full of meaning. John Gill asks why the officers are named again after the horses have been mentioned, and answers that it is to show they provided for the horses too, and to introduce what follows — namely, that their large provision was 'not only for Solomon's family and domestic servants, but for strangers from different parts, who came upon messages to him, or to visit him, and to behold the splendour of his court.' Keil and Delitzsch take the verse as yet a further proof of the blessings of peace: the prefects of v.7 provided for the king and for all who came to the royal table, each in his appointed month, so that nothing was wanting.

The witnesses do not divide, and with so plain a verse they let its two emphases carry the weight. The first is the smoothness of the order: twelve men, each his month, the rotation never breaking, the king's household and guests fed without interruption the year round. The second, and the one the commentators dwell on, is the reach of the table — it was not a private board but an open one, drawing the messengers and visitors of many lands to eat of Solomon's bounty, so that the wisdom and glory noised abroad were confirmed to all who came by the very abundance set before them.

Above all, both witnesses rest on the closing words, 'they lacked nothing.' Gill and Keil alike make this the point of the verse: under the peace God had given and through the order the king had established, want was simply unknown at Solomon's court. The commentators do not moralize the phrase heavily, but they let it echo the shepherd's confession that with the Lord for provider one shall not want (Ps. 23:1); the well-kept table of the peaceable king becomes a small, earthly figure of the fulness that flows where God blesses, a household in which, month after month, nothing at all was lacking.
28:
The provender for the royal horses is described, and the witnesses give the verse to the logistics of empire and to the identity of one obscure animal. John Gill explains that the barley was the horses' provender, the common food of horses in those countries — as Bochart shows from many writers and the Mishnah calls it 'the food of beasts' — while the straw served for their litter; and he recalls that the Roman cavalry too drew a fixed ration of barley for their horses, which they called the hordiarium. Keil and Delitzsch note that barley was the ordinary food of cattle in Palestine and the southern lands, where oats are not cultivated, and that the barley and straw were brought, each officer in his charge, to whatever place the horses happened to be.

The knot is the word rendered 'dromedaries.' Adam Clarke reviews the versions — the Vulgate's 'beasts of burden,' the Syriac and Arabic 'mares,' the Septuagint's 'chariots,' the Chaldee's 'race-horses' — and judges that the Hebrew rechesh means 'a very swift kind of horse,' a race-horse or post-horse; and he draws the practical inference that 'to communicate with so many distant provinces, Solomon had need of many animals of this kind.' Charles Ellicott agrees, taking the word for 'swift beasts,' probably the horses of the royal messengers, as distinguished from the war-horses.

The witnesses differ only in how confidently they name the creature — Clarke ranging through the versions, Ellicott and Gill content with 'swift beasts' or 'a swifter sort' — and they agree on what the verse reveals. Behind the barley and straw stands a whole system of communication: relays of fleet horses carrying the king's word to the ends of his wide dominion, and an administration exact enough that each district's officer knew to bring the feed to the right place at the right time. The commentators let this humble note of fodder complete the picture of Solomon's governance — an empire held together not only by tribute and cavalry but by roads, messengers, and the unglamorous, faithful supply that kept them moving.
29:
The theme now turns from Solomon's wealth to his wisdom, and the witnesses begin where the text does — with its source in God. John Gill insists the wisdom was 'not from the acuteness of his genius merely, nor from his industry and diligence,' but by the gift of God, from whom all solid wisdom flows (Prov. 2:6). Matthew Henry presses the same fountain: God gave it, in answer to prayer and by a special grant of favour, and it embraced a vast compass — 'great knowledge of distant countries and the histories of former times, a quickness of thought, strength of memory, and clearness of judgment, such as never any man had.'

The witnesses then anatomize the threefold gift. Keil and Delitzsch distinguish the terms: chokmah is practical wisdom, the skill to decide what is judicious and useful; tebunah is keenness of understanding to solve difficult and complicated problems; and 'largeness of heart' is the mental capacity to embrace the most diverse departments of knowledge. Henry hears in that 'largeness of heart' both a vast compass of mind and, some think, a courage and a free, communicative disposition to do good with it. Adam Clarke illustrates the same breadth with Lord Bacon's happy figure: 'as the sand on the sea-shore encloses a great body of waters, so Solomon's mind contained an ocean of knowledge.'

Only Charles Ellicott sounds a warning note, and it is the verse's deepest. Understanding and largeness of heart, he says, are but the means; the crown is wisdom, which is properly the attribute of God, reflected in man by revelation, so that 'the fear of the Lord' is its beginning and faith the supplement of its necessary imperfection. Should that sense of dependence on God be lost, this very consciousness of wisdom 'may well become a self-idolatry,' the mind priding itself on having pierced the secret of being. The witnesses do not contradict one another; laid together, their readings become almost prophetic. All agree the gift is God's and marvellous; but Ellicott, knowing how Solomon's story ends, quietly marks that wisdom severed from the fear of the Lord turns in upon itself — so that the chapter's praise of the wisest of men carries within it the warning of his fall.
30:
Solomon's wisdom is now measured against the famed learning of the nations, and the witnesses identify the rivals it surpassed. John Gill takes 'the children of the east' for the Arabians and Persians, famous for a wisdom that lay in the knowledge of the stars and in divination, and the wisdom of Egypt as their great western counterpart. Keil and Delitzsch define the phrase more exactly: the sons of the East are the Arabian tribes stretching toward the Euphrates — the Sabaeans and the Temanites, celebrated for their gnomic wisdom (Jeremiah 49:7; Obadiah 1:8) — but the word 'all' must also draw in the Chaldeans, renowned for their astronomy and astrology. Adam Clarke gathers the same peoples, the Chaldeans, Persians, and Arabians, who with the Egyptians 'were famed for wisdom and knowledge through all the world.'

Charles Ellicott gives the comparison its depth by pointing to a living witness. The 'children of the east' are the tribes of the country between Israel and Mesopotamia, and of these Job is expressly said to be one, and among the chief (Job 1:3); 'what their wisdom was, the utterances of Job and his friends may testify,' showing large knowledge of nature and of man, speculating on the deepest moral questions, and resting through all upon the consciousness of the one God. This eastern wisdom he links with the proverbial wisdom of Egypt, which the monuments show to have been part of an advanced and elaborate civilization, rich in learning and art, though perhaps 'less free and vigorous than the simpler patriarchal wisdom of the children of the east.'

The witnesses do not dispute; their pooled learning serves a single purpose. By naming the genuine glories of the ancient mind — the star-lore of Chaldea, the schools of Egypt, the grave meditations of Job's world — they measure the height of Solomon's gift, which excelled them all. And the point, as Matthew Henry had drawn it from the same passage, is not merely comparative but theological: 'if nature excels art, much more does grace,' for the knowledge God gives by special favour goes beyond all that man attains by his own labour. Solomon standing above the sages of the East is thus, for the commentators, a standing proof of the surpassing worth of wisdom that comes down from above.
31:
Solomon is declared wiser than named men of renown, and the witnesses divide, in a friendly way, over who these four sages were. John Gill keeps to the sweeping claim — wiser than all men since the fall of Adam, especially in natural wisdom of every branch. But the four names, Ethan, Heman, Chalcol, and Darda, the sons of Mahol, set the commentators a puzzle of identity. Keil and Delitzsch hold them 'most probably the same as the sons of Zerach' listed in 1 Chronicles 2:6, since the names agree; they were Levites, Heman a Korahite and grandson of Samuel, Ethan a Merarite, both called Ezrahites in the titles of two Psalms (Ps. 88; Ps. 89).

Charles Ellicott resists that identification. These men were plainly Solomon's contemporaries and rivals in fame, he argues, and so cannot belong to a family of Zerah many generations earlier; the coincidence of names is remarkable but 'can scarcely hold.' He points instead to the Heman and Ethan whom David set over the service of song (1 Chronicles 6:33), Heman being called 'the king's seer in the words of the Lord' (1 Chronicles 25:5), and notes that 'Mahol' may not be a father's name at all but a word meaning 'dance' or 'music,' marking these men, like Heman and Ethan, as masters of the temple's song. Adam Clarke reasons the same way — the sons of Zerah 'flourished long before Solomon's time' and are probably not these — and takes 'sons of Mahol' as 'masters of dancing or music,' a hint of Solomon's own skill in music and poetry.

So the debate is real but gentle. Keil, weighing the names, joins the four to the ancient line of Zerah; Ellicott and Clarke, weighing the word 'contemporary,' unhitch them from that line and set them among David's own temple singers, reading 'Mahol' as their art rather than their sire. The witnesses agree entirely on the substance the identification serves: that Solomon towered over the very men whom his age reckoned wisest, so that his fame 'was in all nations round about.' Whether those rivals were the sages of an older Judah or the inspired musicians of the sanctuary, the point stands unshaken — the wisest heads of Israel were themselves surpassed by the son of David.
32:
The fruits of the wisdom are counted — three thousand proverbs and a thousand and five songs — and the witnesses reflect on how vast the output was and how little of it survives. John Gill distinguishes these spoken proverbs from the canonical book: they were wise, pithy sentences taken from his lips and spread abroad, which 'in process of time were lost,' whereas the Book of Proverbs, written under divine inspiration, is preserved. Keil and Delitzsch and Adam Clarke reckon the loss precisely — of the three thousand proverbs only a selection remains in the canonical book, and of the thousand and five songs only the Song of Songs, with perhaps two Psalms bearing his name (Ps. 72; Ps. 127).

Charles Ellicott turns the count into a meditation on the canon. The word mashal, from a root meaning 'comparison,' covers parable, proverb, riddle, and antithetical poem; the Book of Proverbs preserves chiefly two collected series (Proverbs 10–24; 25–29), far short of the three thousand. This shortfall he finds full of significance: 'the Old Testament Canon is not a collection of chance fragments of a scanty literature,' but a deliberate selection, made 'by prophetic authority' out of a large and copious body of writing — the men of Hezekiah, by Jewish tradition Isaiah and his companions, gathering what was fit (Proverbs 25:1). Much of Solomon's poetry, he adds, may have been purely secular, and his later declension into idolatry would well induce care in the choosing.

The witnesses do not divide; their notes combine into one striking conclusion. That so great a treasury of proverb and song should have almost wholly perished, leaving only the inspired remnant, is for Gill the difference between what man's memory keeps and what God's Spirit secures, and for Ellicott the proof that the Scriptures are a sifted and guarded selection rather than the whole surviving library of Israel. Together they read the verse as a quiet doctrine of providence over the canon: out of the thousands Solomon spoke, God preserved exactly what his people needed, and let the rest fall away.
33:
Solomon's wisdom is shown ranging over the whole of nature, from the cedar to the hyssop, and the witnesses read the verse as at once a survey of creation and a lost book of science. John Gill takes it broadly — he spoke of all trees, herbs, and plants, of their nature, virtues, and use. Keil and Delitzsch catch the art of the phrasing: the cedar and the hyssop are set in deliberate antithesis, the greatest and most glorious of trees against the smallest and most insignificant of plants, precisely to embrace the whole vegetable kingdom between them.

Adam Clarke mourns what is gone. Here, he says, is 'a complete system of natural history, as far as relates to the animal and vegetable kingdoms,' and Solomon 'was probably the first natural historian in the world'; and he grieves that these works are 'all and for ever lost,' imagining how the hearts of Ray, Linnaeus, Buffon, and Cuvier must be wrung to know it — yet bowing to the providence of God that did not think fit to preserve them. Charles Ellicott surveys the interpretations that have gathered round the verse: Josephus, who thought Solomon spoke of these things allegorically yet 'like a philosopher'; the rabbinic and Oriental legends of magical and occult knowledge; and the modern reading that finds here 'the first dawn of a scientific natural history.' In each, he judges, there is some truth. But the Books of Proverbs, Ecclesiastes, and the Song, and much of the Psalms and of Job, show that Solomon's science was never mere cataloguing: it joined a keen observation of nature to a constant contemplation of God in and over nature, as in that psalm which sings the works of creation (Psalms 104), and so tended always toward a sense of unity and a mystic parable.

The witnesses do not sharply contend, though they weigh the character of Solomon's learning differently — Clarke seeing a lost empirical science, Ellicott a wisdom that read every creature back to its Maker. Their agreement is the larger thing. From the cedar of Lebanon to the moss on the wall, from the beasts to the fishes, the wisest of men surveyed the whole compass of the living world; and the commentators find in that survey not a rival to faith but its handmaid — a mind so gifted that it could trace the finger of God across the entire range of his creatures, and yet, as the chapter's praise everywhere implies, a gift held in trust, to be used in the fear of the Lord who gave it.
34:
The chapter ends with the nations streaming to hear Solomon, and the witnesses read the scene as the crown of his fame and the shadow of a greater King. John Gill records simply that there came of all people to hear his wisdom. Keil and Delitzsch explain that the widespread report brought many strangers to Jerusalem, the more so because such wisdom was rare among princes; and they point forward to the queen of Sheba's coming (1 Kings 10) as the historical proof, noting how far later ages, from Josephus to the Koran, embroidered the story with tales of magical books. Adam Clarke agrees that the queen of Sheba was one such visitant and the most fully recorded, and supposes that the people who came from all kings of the earth were chiefly ambassadors, sent to form and maintain friendship with so renowned a king.

The Puritan Matthew Henry lifts the verse to its height and to its Lord. Solomon's court, he says, 'was the staple of learning, and the rendezvous of philosophers,' who came from all parts 'to light their candle at his lamp.' But the resort of the nations to the wise king is for Henry chiefly a type: Solomon 'was herein a type of Christ, in whom are hid all the treasures of wisdom and knowledge' (Colossians 2:3) — 'and hid for us, for he is made of God to us wisdom.' As the world once came to Solomon, so 'Christ's fame shall spread through all the earth, and men of all nations shall come to him, learn of him, and take upon them his easy yoke, and find rest for their souls.'

The witnesses do not divide; they end where the chapter has been tending all along. The historical fact — kings and ambassadors drawn from every quarter to the wisdom of Jerusalem — is real and, in the queen of Sheba, documented; but in the hands of the commentators it opens beyond itself. The nations gathering to the son of David to hear wisdom is the earthly shadow of the nations gathering to great David's greater Son, in whom the treasures of wisdom are hidden not to be admired but to be shared. So the register of Solomon's glory closes, for these expositors, not in mere wonder at a dead king's fame, but in a summons to come to the living Wisdom of God.
